The practice of compelling an individual to act against their will by using force, threats, intimidation, or extreme dependency, often for personal or financial gain.

The process of socializing an individual, often through psychological manipulation, to adopt the beliefs and goals of a violent extremist group and become willing to engage in activities that support the group.
The process of deliberately building an emotional connection with a person to lower their inhibitions for the purpose of sexual abuse, exploitation, or trafficking.
A pattern of unwanted, intrusive behavior (physical, verbal, or online) directed at a specific person that causes fear, distress, or emotional harm.
The act of recruiting, transporting, transferring, harboring, or receiving a person through force, fraud, or coercion for the purpose of exploitation, such as forced labor or sexual servitude.
Any other incident involving manipulation or coercion of people for harmful purposes that does not fit into the specific abuse subcategories.
An incident involving legitimate, approved access to resources that may be relevant for auditing, monitoring, or establishing a baseline of normal activity.
The installation or discovery of a concealed method of bypassing normal authentication or encryption in a computer system, product, or embedded device.
The act of creating an unauthorized copy of a physical or digital security credential, such as an access card, to gain illicit entry to a facility or system.
An incident where a legitimate user's credentials (e.g., username/password) are stolen or guessed and used by an unauthorized individual to gain access to systems or data.
An incident where a user or process gains access rights, permissions, or capabilities that exceed those normally assigned, often to bypass security restrictions.
An incident involving the use of physical force to breach a barrier (e.g., lock, door, window) or the repeated systematic guessing of passwords to gain unauthorized access.
An incident where physical or digital access credentials (e.g., keys, ID badges, login details) are misplaced, stolen, or used by an unauthorized person.
A physical security breach where an unauthorized person follows an authorized individual through a secured entry point, circumventing access control mechanisms.
An incident involving physical entry into a restricted building, room, or area without proper authorization.
Any other incident related to physical or digital access that does not fit into the specific access subcategories.
An incident where multiple compromised systems (a botnet) are used to target a single system with a flood of traffic, causing a denial of service.
An incident where a single machine or network attempts to make a system, service, or network resource unavailable by overwhelming it with malicious requests or traffic.
An incident caused by the unintentional malfunction of hardware or software due to errors, bugs, wear and tear, or other faults, leading to service degradation or unavailability.
A periodic signal generated by hardware or software to indicate normal operation, often used for monitoring system health, connectivity, or location tracking.
An incident where incorrect configuration of systems, software, or networks leads to service disruptions, outages, or security vulnerabilities.
An incident where essential utilities or services (such as electricity, water, or network connectivity) become unavailable, disrupting normal operations.
An incident where a system or component is subjected to a load beyond its designed capacity, leading to performance degradation or failure, even if the load is not malicious.
Any other incident that impacts the availability of resources or services, not covered by the specific subcategories.
An incident where animals pose a direct threat to human safety, public health (e.g., zoonotic diseases), or economic stability (e.g., livestock diseases).
The rapid and widespread occurrence of an infectious disease in a specific population or region, exceeding what is normally expected.
An incident involving a harmful outbreak or infestation of insects that threatens public health, agriculture, livestock, or property.
A fictional or hypothetical scenario involving a pathogen that causes a pandemic of aggressive, infectious behavior, often used as a metaphor for worst-case outbreak scenarios in planning.
Any other incident caused bybiological not covered by specific subcategories.
A prolonged period of below-average precipitation leading to a water shortage, which can impact agriculture, ecosystems, and water supplies.
An incident where a glacial lake dam (often moraine or ice) fails, rapidly releasing a large volume of water and causing devastating floods downstream.
An unplanned and uncontrolled fire burning in natural or rural areas, often exacerbated by climatic conditions like drought, wind, and heat.
Any other incident caused by long-lived atmospheric processes (climatological) not covered by specific subcategories.
A hypothetical incident involving the discovery of or interaction with extraterrestrial intelligent life, a theoretical scenario in scientific and security planning.
An incident involving a celestial object (asteroid, meteoroid, comet) colliding with Earth, potentially causing localized or global damage.
An event caused by solar or cosmic activity, such as solar flares or geomagnetic storms, that can disrupt Earth's technological infrastructure.
Any other incident caused extraterrestrial process not covered by specific subcategories.
The act of reproducing, distributing, or installing software, media, or other materials in violation of their copyright, often for personal gain or distribution (piracy).
A fraudulent scheme that is made possible by the abuse of power or position by a trusted individual (e.g., employee, official) who acts for personal gain.
The use of illegal or unethical means, such as hacking, bribery, or theft, to acquire a competitor's trade secrets, intellectual property, or other confidential business information.
A type of attack where an attacker illegitimately assumes the identity of another user, process, or system to gain unauthorized access, privileges, or benefits.

A cyber attack where an attacker disguises themselves as a trustworthy entity (e.g., via email or fake website) to trick a victim into revealing sensitive information like usernames, passwords, or credit card details.
The use of an organization's assets (e.g., computing power, network, email) for non-work-related, often illegal, activities without authorization.
Any other incident involving deception for financial or reputational gain that does not fit into the specific fraud subcategories.
An incident caused by a sudden, rapid shaking of the earth resulting from the movement of tectonic plates, which can cause ground shaking, surface rupture, and tsunamis.
An incident involving the downslope movement of rock, soil, or snow under the force of gravity, such as landslides, avalanches, or rockfalls.
Any other incident caused by solid-earth processes not covered by specific geophysical subcategories.
An incident caused by the eruption of magma and volcanic gases from a volcano, which can produce lava flows, pyroclastic flows, ashfall, and lahars.
An incident where water submerges land that is normally dry, often caused by heavy rain, storm surge, or dam failure, leading to property damage and risk to life.
An incident involving the downward movement of slope materials (soil, rock) triggered by water saturation from heavy rain or snowmelt.
An incident involving destructive waves, such as tsunamis or storm surges, that can cause coastal flooding, erosion, and damage.
Any other incident caused by the movement, distribution, and quality of water, not covered by specific hydrological subcategories.
A security incident caused by a current or former employee, contractor, or other trusted insider who intentionally acts to harm the organization, its data, or its people.
A security incident caused unintentionally by an insider, such as through carelessness, lack of awareness, or simple human error, leading to data exposure or system compromise.
Any other security incident involving an insider (trusted individual) that does not fit into the specific insider threat subcategories.
A type of software that automatically displays or downloads unwanted advertisements, often in a disruptive manner, and may track user behavior.
A type of malware that bypasses normal authentication to give an attacker persistent remote access to a compromised system, often enabling data theft or installation of additional malware.
A type of malware that secretly uses a victim's computing resources to mine cryptocurrency, causing degraded performance and increased power consumption without consent.
A type of malware designed to download and install other malicious software from a remote server, typically acting as the first stage in a multi-step attack.
A type of malware that encrypts a victim's files or systems, rendering them inaccessible, and demands a ransom payment, often in cryptocurrency, for the decryption key.
A type of malware designed to hide its presence and grant an attacker persistent, privileged access to a compromised system while evading detection.
A type of software that secretly monitors and collects information about a user's activities, such as keystrokes, browsing habits, and personal data, without their consent.
A type of malware that disguises itself as legitimate or desirable software to trick users into installing it, after which it can perform malicious actions.
A type of malicious software that attaches itself to a legitimate program or file and replicates itself to spread to other programs, often requiring human action to propagate.
A type of standalone malicious software that replicates itself to spread across networks, often exploiting vulnerabilities without requiring human interaction.
Any other incident involving malicious software that does not fit into the specific malware subcategories.
A meteorological incident involving a rapid fall in temperature over a short period, or a prolonged period of extreme cold, posing risks to health and infrastructure.
A meteorological incident where dense fog reduces visibility, potentially disrupting transportation and causing accidents.
A meteorological incident involving a prolonged period of excessively hot weather, which can cause health impacts, infrastructure stress, and environmental damage.
A meteorological incident involving excessive or prolonged rainfall that can lead to flooding, landslides, and transportation disruptions.
A meteorological incident involving heavy snowfall and blizzard conditions, which can disrupt transportation, damage infrastructure, and pose risks to safety.
A meteorological incident involving damaging or dangerous winds, such as from storms, tornadoes, or hurricanes, that can cause structural damage and power outages.
Any other incident caused by atmospheric processes not covered by specific meteorological subcategories.
A state of armed conflict between two or more nations, involving organized military forces and impacting national security.
Large-scale illegal activities, such as trafficking in drugs, weapons, or people, conducted by sophisticated criminal networks that pose a threat to national and international security.
Large-scale cyber operations, including attacks, espionage, and disinformation campaigns, conducted by state-sponsored or state-affiliated groups against national interests.
An incident involving significant disruption to a nation's economy, such as market crashes, hyperinflation, or trade wars, affecting national stability.
Incidents related to large-scale environmental shifts, such as climate change, resource scarcity, or global pandemics, that have significant national security implications.
An incident involving large-scale public disorder, such as strikes, riots, or civil unrest, that challenges social order and may require national-level response.
An incident involving the use of violence, intimidation, or threats by non-state actors against civilians or property to achieve political, religious, or ideological objectives.
Any other incident that has a significant impact at the national scale, not covered by specific national security subcategories.
The use of an organization's assets (e.g., computers, network, time) for purposes that are not officially authorized, which may violate policy but not be malicious.
An incident where an individual fails to comply with established organizational policies, procedures, or security rules, whether intentionally or accidentally.
An incident where a designed process, procedure, or workflow fails to achieve its intended outcome, leading to operational or security gaps.
Any other operational issue that does not fit into the specific operational incident subcategories.
The act of using aerial platforms, such as drones or satellites, to conduct surveillance or gather intelligence about a target area.
The act of physically observing a location to gather information about security measures, layouts, or potential vulnerabilities.
The practice of probing a network to discover active hosts, open ports, and services, often as a precursor to an attack or unauthorized monitoring.
The practice of collecting and analyzing information from publicly available sources (e.g., social media, public records) for intelligence purposes.
Any other incident involving scanning, spying, or monitoring to identify resources that does not fit into specific recon subcategories.
The intentional act of deleting, altering, or corrupting digital or physical data to cause harm, disrupt operations, or cover tracks.
The intentional and malicious act of destroying or severely damaging physical assets, such as windows, equipment, or buildings.
The intentional disruption of essential services or utilities, such as electricity, water, or network connectivity, to cause operational downtime.
The intentional act of causing damage to operational equipment, machinery, or vehicles, often to disrupt production or operations.
The act of willfully defacing, damaging, or marking public or private property with inscriptions, drawings, or tags without permission.
The act of deliberately meddling with or disabling security controls (e.g., locks, alarms, cameras) to compromise their effectiveness.
The deliberate and malicious act of damaging, destroying, or obstructing an organization's physical assets, operations, or systems.
Any other incident involving the intentional damage to property or assets not covered by the specific sabotage subcategories.
An unplanned, unforeseen event (e.g., vehicle crash, industrial mishap, chemical spill) that results in injury, loss of life, or damage to health.
An incident where an individual uses physical force against another person, causing bodily harm, pain, or the fear of immediate harm.
A sudden, violent release of energy (e.g., from gas, chemicals, or explosives) that causes a blast, fire, and potential structural damage, injury, or loss of life.
An incident involving uncontrolled burning (e.g., structural, wildland, or chemical fire) that threatens human safety, property, or the environment.
An incident where a person or group is held against their will by a captor, often to compel a third party to meet certain demands.
An incident involving any unwanted sexual act, contact, or behavior directed against an individual without their consent.
Any other incident that causes or has the potential to cause injury, loss of life, or endanger citizens, not covered by specific safety subcategories.
A social engineering attack that lures victims by offering something desirable (e.g., free music, a prize) in exchange for information or by tricking them into downloading malware.
A type of social engineering where attackers send fraudulent emails, appearing to be from a legitimate source, to trick recipients into revealing sensitive information or installing malware.
A social engineering tactic where the attacker creates a fabricated scenario or pretends to be someone they are not to engage a victim and extract information or access.
A social engineering tactic where the attacker offers a service or benefit (e.g., technical support) in exchange for information or access, often expecting something in return later.
A social engineering attack conducted via SMS (text message), where the attacker sends a fraudulent message to trick the recipient into clicking a malicious link or providing information.
A highly targeted phishing attack directed at a specific individual, organization, or role, often using personalized information to increase credibility.
A social engineering attack conducted over the phone, where the attacker impersonates a legitimate entity to trick the victim into revealing sensitive information or performing actions.
A security incident where an attacker exploits a vulnerability in a third-party vendor's system to gain access to or compromise the primary target's network or data.
An event that disrupts the normal flow of products, services, or information within a supply chain, often impacting operations and delivery.
Any other incident affecting the supply chain that does not fit into the specific supply chain subcategories.
Any other incident involving psychological manipulation of people to divulge information or perform actions, not covered by specific social engineering subcategories.
A security incident involving the unauthorized access, acquisition, or disclosure of sensitive, protected, or confidential data.
The unauthorized taking or copying of sensitive or confidential documents, whether in physical or digital form.
The unlawful taking of physical hardware, such as computers, mobile phones, or servers, resulting in loss of assets and potentially the data they contain.
The theft of financial information, such as credit card numbers or bank account details, often for the purpose of fraudulent transactions.
The unlawful acquisition of a company's intellectual property, including trade secrets, patents, formulas, or proprietary processes.
The unlawful taking of heavy equipment, vehicles, or specialized machinery, often resulting in significant operational and financial loss.
The unauthorized acquisition of Personally Identifiable Information (PII) that can be used to identify, contact, or impersonate an individual.
Any other incident involving the intentional stealing of physical property or digital data not covered by specific theft subcategories.
Any incident that does not fit into one of the predefined categories in this taxonomy.
An incident whose category is currently unknown, under investigation, or cannot be determined.
An incident generated solely for the purpose of testing systems, processes, or training personnel.
A value used to indicate that this attribute is extended and the actual value is provided using the corresponding ext-* attribute. (see :ref:private_extension_enum)
Bastion Host - Secure remote access gateway
Dynamic Application Security Testing - Runtime application testing
Interactive Application Security Testing - Hybrid application testing
Static Application Security Testing - Source code analysis
Cloud Access Security Broker - Cloud service security mediation
Cloud Infrastructure Entitlement Management - Cloud permission management
Cloud Security Posture Management - Cloud configuration monitoring
Cloud Workload Protection Platform - Cloud workload security
Distributed Denial of Service Protection - DDoS mitigation system
Scrubber/Scrubbing Center - Traffic cleaning for DDoS
Web Application Firewall with DDoS - Integrated DDoS protection
Domain-based Message Authentication - Email authentication monitoring
Secure Email Gateway - Comprehensive email security
Endpoint Data Loss Prevention - Endpoint data leakage prevention
Endpoint Detection and Response - Advanced endpoint threat hunting
Endpoint Protection Platform - Comprehensive endpoint security
Host Intrusion Detection System - Host-based threat monitoring
Host Intrusion Prevention System - Host-based threat prevention
Runtime Application Self-Protection - In-app runtime protection
Data-Centric Audit and Protection - Data-centric security monitoring
Data Loss Prevention - Data leakage prevention across channels
Identity and Access Management - Identity governance and access control
Identity Risk Management - Identity-based risk analysis
Privileged Access Management - Privileged access management
Privileged Identity Management - Privileged account security
User and Entity Behavior Analytics - Behavioral threat detection
Deep Packet Inspection - Advanced packet analysis
Firewall - Network traffic filtering and policy enforcement
Network Access Control - Endpoint compliance and access enforcement
Network Behavior Anomaly Detection - Anomaly detection in network behavior
Network Detection and Response - Advanced network threat hunting
Next-Generation Firewall - Advanced firewall with app awareness
Network Intrusion Detection System - Network traffic analysis for threats
Network Intrusion Prevention System - Inline threat prevention
Proxy Server - ACL and TLS session monitoring
Web Application Firewall - HTTP/HTTPS traffic filtering
Wireless Intrusion Detection System - WiFi threat detection
Wireless Intrusion Prevention System - WiFi threat prevention
IoT Intrusion Detection System - IoT device threat detection
Operational Technology IDS - Industrial control system monitoring
1D Laser Sensor - Basic laser presence/distance detection
1D Light Detection and Ranging Sensor - Single-beam laser for distance measurement
2D Light Detection and Ranging Sensor - Planar laser scanning for 2D mapping
3D Light Detection and Ranging Sensor - 3D environmental scanning and mapping
Access Control System - Physical entry/exit control monitoring
Anti-Drone System - Drone detection and countermeasure system
Face Recognition Camera - Biometric facial recognition system
Glass Break Detector - Acoustic glass breakage detection
Human Activity Recognition - AI-based human behavior and motion analysis
Object Detection Camera - General object detection and classification
Voice Activity Detection - Audio analysis for voice/speech detection
Visible and Near-Infrared - Multi-spectral imaging sensor
Extract, Transform, Load - Data pipeline tools (Logstash, Fluentd, Vector)
Log Analyzer - Log aggregation and analysis (e.g., ELK Stack, Splunk)
Network Management System - Network monitoring and management
Security Information and Event Management - Centralized security logging and alerting
Security Orchestration and Response - Automated incident response
Cyber Threat Intelligence - Strategic threat intelligence
Threat Intelligence Feed - External threat data streams
Threat Intelligence Platform - Threat data aggregation and analysis
Attack Surface Management - External attack surface monitoring
Penetration Testing Tools - Manual/automated security testing
A value used to indicate that this attribute is extended and the actual value is provided using the corresponding ext-* attribute. (see )
summary reports, alerts from other systems
A value used to indicate that this attribute is extended and the actual value is provided using the corresponding ext-* attribute. (see )
An analyzer that uses machine learning, deep learning, or other artificial intelligence techniques to learn normal behavior and detect sophisticated or novel threats.
An analyzer that identifies deviations from established norms or baselines without relying on predefined signatures, often flagging unusual patterns in traffic, behavior, or system activity.
An analyzer that monitors and analyzes the actions of users, entities, or processes over time to detect malicious or suspicious activities that deviate from expected behavior patterns.
An analyzer that uses electronic devices to capture and measure unique physical or behavioral characteristics (e.g., fingerprint, iris, voice) for identification or authentication purposes.
A method that analyses traffic destined for a non-existent or sinkhole route to identify malicious activity, such as connections to known command-and-control servers or scanning from infected hosts.
An analyzer that enriches raw events with additional context (e.g., asset value, user role, time of day, business criticality) to prioritize alerts and assess true impact.
An analyzer that aggregates and examines multiple, disparate data streams or events to identify complex relationships, patterns, sequences, or dependencies that indicate a security incident.
An analyzer that combines multiple detection methods (e.g., signature, anomaly, behavioral) to improve accuracy, reduce false positives, and detect threats that single methods might miss.
An analyzer that creates unique identifiers or "fingerprints" for devices, applications, or network stacks to detect spoofing, unauthorized devices, or configuration changes.
An analyzer that detects incidents based on the rate or regularity of events (e.g., repeated failed logins, rapid-fire requests) exceeding or falling below expected frequencies.
An analyzer that combines data from multiple heterogeneous sensors and sources to create a comprehensive, high-confidence view of an incident, reducing ambiguity and false alerts.
An analyzer that determines the physical location of an asset or event (e.g., login attempt, IP address, device) and flags activities occurring from unexpected or high-risk locations.
An analyzer that models relationships between entities (users, devices, processes) as graphs and detects anomalies or attack paths by analyzing connections, dependencies, and traversals.
An analyzer (sensor or device) that detects, measures, and monitors thermal energy (infrared radiation) to identify anomalies like fires, overheating equipment, or human presence.
An analyzer that detects potentially unknown threats by using algorithmic logic, rules of thumb, or suspicious characteristics rather than relying on specific signature matches.
A decoy system or resource designed to lure, detect, and analyze malicious activity by mimicking a legitimate target, diverting attackers away from real assets.
An analyzer that continuously checks systems and configurations against security best practices, compliance standards, or hardening guidelines to identify weaknesses or drift.
An analyzer that monitors critical system components (files, configurations, registry keys) for unauthorized changes, verifying their integrity against a known good baseline.
An analyzer that examines the data about data (e.g., file creation timestamps, email headers, connection logs) to uncover hidden relationships or suspicious attributes.
An analyzer that continuously observes a system, network, or environment to track its state, health, or activity, often providing real-time alerts on specific conditions.
An analyzer (sensor or system) that detects, tracks, and quantifies physical motion using technologies like radar, lidar, or video analytics.
An analyzer that coordinates and triggers automated response actions based on detected incidents, often integrated with SOAR (Security Orchestration, Automation, and Response) platforms.
An analyzer that identifies specific sequences, combinations, or recurring arrangements of events or data that indicate malicious activity, even if individual elements appear benign.
An analyzer that evaluates events, configurations, or behaviors against a set of predefined rules, configurations, or compliance requirements to detect violations or misconfigurations.
An analyzer that uses historical data and modeling to forecast potential future incidents, vulnerabilities, or attack vectors before they occur.
An analyzer that validates network traffic or communications against expected protocol specifications, RFC compliance, or standard behavior to detect anomalies or malicious variations.
An analyzer that actively or passively probes or monitors an environment to discover assets, services, or vulnerabilities, often as part of a defensive assessment or adversary simulation.
An analyzer that evaluates the trustworthiness of an entity (e.g., IP address, domain, file hash) by checking it against known threat intelligence lists, blocklists, or reputation scores.
An analyzer that applies conditional logic (if-then-else statements) defined by experts to correlate events and generate alerts based on specific combinations of conditions.
An analyzer that detects threats by examining the order and timing of events, identifying attack chains or kill chain progressions (e.g., scan → exploit → installation → C2).
An analyzer that detects known threats by matching events or patterns against a database of specific signatures, hashes, or Indicators of Compromise (IoCs).
An analyzer that detects anomalies by establishing a baseline of normal behavior and identifying events that deviate significantly from expected statistical parameters.
A mechanism that intentionally slows down or delays suspicious connections (e.g., network connections or login attempts) to hinder automated attacks and scanning.
An analyzer that ingests and matches internal events against external threat feeds, IoC lists, and adversary TTPs (Tactics, Techniques, and Procedures) to identify known threats.
An analyzer that detects incidents by comparing a metric or count (e.g., number of failed logins, traffic volume) against a predefined limit or threshold.
An analyzer that monitors data over extended periods to identify gradual changes, emerging patterns, or long-term shifts that may indicate evolving threats or security degradation.
A value used to indicate that this attribute is extended and the actual value is provided using the corresponding ext-* attribute. (see )
Ultrasonic noise (for pest detection or leak detection)
CWA (Chemical Warfare Agents - Nerve gas, Blister agents)
Volatile Organic Compounds (VOCs) - paint fumes, off-gassing
Malicious code injected into a database query (SQL Injection).
The script injected into a webpage to steal data or redirect users.
A compromised key used to access cloud resources (AWS, Azure) or SaaS platforms.
The specific cloud storage location where data was stored or exfiltrated from.
The unique ID of the compromised virtual machine or database.
A malicious third-party app granted permissions to a user's data.
The authentication result showing if the email passed security checks.
A unique identifier for the email used to track it across mail servers.
The target email address of the attack.
The address where replies go; often different from the sender for tracking.
The address where non-delivery receipts go; used in spoofing analysis.
The "From" address used in a phishing campaign.
The original IP address of the sender (if recorded by the mail server).
The exact command line used to execute the malware; often reveals attacker intent.
The name of a malicious library loaded into a legitimate process (DLL Sideloading).
The cryptographic fingerprint of the malicious file (SHA256, MD5).
The location on the file system where the malware resides.
Code or processes that exist only in RAM (volatile memory) and not on disk.
The unique identifier of the malicious process at the time of analysis.
The name of the malicious executable file running on the endpoint.
The Windows Registry path used for persistence or configuration changes.
The name of a task created by the attacker for persistence or execution.
The name of a malicious service installed to run the malware.
Metadata about the connection (who talked to whom, when, and data volume).
The raw data being transmitted containing passwords or exfiltrated data.
The unique identifier of a specific compromised laptop or mobile device.
The specific make/model/serial of a USB drive used to introduce malware.
The specific time of an unusual login (e.g., 3:00 AM from a foreign location).
The specific user account that was compromised or used for lateral movement.
The source IP of an attacker, a Command & Control (C2) server, or a malicious scan.
The source IP in IPv6 format; increasingly used to bypass IPv4-based allowlists.
The physical device identifier on a local network; useful if the attacker is on-site.
The specific source or destination port used (e.g., 445 for ransomware spreading).
The network protocol used; anomalous use may indicate tunneling (e.g., DNS tunneling).
The domain hosting malware or Command & Control infrastructure (e.g., evil.com).
Unusual HTTP methods like PUT or DELETE used to manipulate data.
The page the user was on before clicking the malicious link.
A hijacked session token used to impersonate a user without a password.
The specific directory or file requested on the web server.
The full malicious link pointing to a payload or phishing page.
The browser or application signature used by the attacker when connecting.
Gas leaks (specific gases absorb specific IR wavelengths)
Warm bodies (humans, animals) via Passive Infrared (PIR)
Forgery (detecting UV security features on documents)
Biological signatures (retinas, irises) - Failed recognition indicates intrusion attempt
Motion (optical flow, changes in pixel arrays)
Specific objects (via computer vision: faces, vehicles, text, barcodes)
Water pressure (for leak detection via pressure drop)
Physical contact (bumpers on robots, touch screens)
Current flow (Hall effect sensors, clamp meters)
Orientation (Compass / Magnetometer - detecting Earth's magnetic field)
License plates (Automatic Number Plate Recognition - ANPR)
Chemical spills (specific conductivity or pH changes)
Level of liquid in a tank (ultrasonic, capacitive, float)
Explosion (Sudden light flash + pressure wave + sound)
Failed retina/iris scan (multiple failures may indicate intrusion)
Fire (Combination of smoke, heat, and flicker)
A value used to indicate that this attribute is extended and the actual value is provided using the corresponding ext-* attribute. (see )
Buried water, gas, electricity, fiber networks
Market studies, ad campaigns, social media
Unbuilt land, forests, or agricultural grounds
A value used to indicate that this attribute is extended and the actual value is provided using the corresponding ext-* attribute. (see )

section.name: Examples
section.t: This section contains several examples of events/incidents which may be described using the IDMEF Data Model defined in.
For each example, the serialization method listed in Section 5 was used on the original IDMEF message to produce a JSON representation.

section.name: Physical intrusion
section.t: Listing 1 describes an incident where an unidentified man was detected on company premises near the building where server room A is located.

section.name: Cyberattack
section.t: Listing 2 describes an incident related to a potential bruteforce attack against the "root" user account of the server at 192.0.2.2 and 2001:db8::/32.

section.name: Server outage
section.t: Listing 3 describes an incident where the webserver at "www.example.com" encountered some kind of failure condition resulting in an outage.
section.sourcecode: { "Version": "2.D.V07", "ID": "819df7bc-35ef-40d8-bbee-1901117370b3", "Description": "A server did not reply to an ICMP ping request", "Type": "Availability", "Priority": "Medium", "Status": "Incident", "Cause": "Unknown", "CreateTime": "2021-05-10T16:59:11.875209+00:00", "StartTime": "2021-05-10T16:59:11.875209+00:00", "Category": [ "Availability.Outage" ], "Analyzer": { "Name": "NMS", "Hostname": "nms.example.com", "Model": "Concerto NMS 5.2", "Category": [ "SIEM.NMS" ], "Data": [ "Network" ], "Method": [ "Monitor" ], "IP": "192.0.2.1" }, "Target": [ { "IP": "192.168.1.2", "Hostname": "www.acme.com", "Service": "website", "Location": "Server room A1, rack 10" } ] }

section.name: Combined incident
section.t: Listing 4 describes a combined incident resulting from the correlation of the previous physical, cyber and availability incidents.

section.name: Hazard incident
section.t: Listing 5 describes a heavy snow storm announced in 48h on Paris and Versailles.

section.name: JSON Validation Schema (Non-normative)
section.t: Listing 5 contains a JSON Schema that can be used to validate incoming IDMEF messages prior to processing. Please note that extraneous linebreaks have been included due to formatting constraints.
